does: This paper introduces the Correlated Knowledge Attack Agent (CKA-Agent), a novel jailbreak methodology that bypasses LLM safety guardrails by decomposing harmful queries into sequences of benign sub-queries that individually evade detection. Rather than optimizing a single malicious prompt, the approach weaves together multiple innocuous queries guided by an adaptive tree-search algorithm that explores the target model's knowledge base. The method leverages the interconnected structure of LLM internal knowledge, where harmful objectives can be reconstructed from aggregated responses to seemingly harmless intermediate questions. The attack demonstrates that modern guardrails designed to detect direct malicious prompts remain vulnerable when harmful information is reconstructed through chains of benign interactions.
connects: This work extends the long-standing literature on adversarial attacks against LLMs and jailbreaking techniques, moving beyond the dominant prompt-optimization paradigm exemplified by methods like AutoPrompt and agent-based searches. It responds to recent observations that semantic guardrails have become increasingly effective at detecting directly malicious prompts, thereby identifying a conceptual gap in existing defense mechanisms. The paper connects to research on prompt injection, multi-turn attacks, and the structural properties of LLM knowledge representations, while building on tree-search and reinforcement learning approaches for exploring model behavior. It also relates to work examining how benign sequences can combine to produce unsafe outputs, and contributes to the broader understanding of emergent vulnerabilities arising from the distributed nature of knowledge in large models.
enables: This research points toward several important future directions in both offensive and defensive AI security. It motivates the development of more sophisticated guardrails that detect harmful information synthesis across multi-turn conversations, rather than only screening individual prompts. The findings suggest future work in analyzing and reorganizing LLM internal knowledge structures to resist decomposition attacks, and in designing training methods that prevent harmful knowledge from being reconstructible through benign queries. The framework also enables investigation of how defense mechanisms must operate at the level of information assembly rather than prompt detection alone, potentially spurring new approaches to safety alignment that account for knowledge graph vulnerabilities and multi-step reasoning patterns.
